name: Resveratrol
historicalUse: Resveratrol-rich plants including Polygonum cuspidatum (Japanese knotweed) and grape vine have been used in Chinese and Japanese traditional medicine for centuries. Itadori (Polygonum cuspidatum) is a classical remedy in Chinese herbalism for inflammatory conditions, urinary support, and longevity. Grape-based preparations appear in Rambam's dietary writings and throughout Arabic medicine as tonics for cardiac and circulatory health.
classicalSources: Bencao Gangmu (Li Shizhen — Chinese Materia Medica)
modernEvidence: Trans-resveratrol is a stilbene polyphenol with potent sirtuin-1 (SIRT1) activating properties. It mimics caloric restriction at the molecular level, activating longevity-associated pathways including SIRT1/AMPK/mTOR signaling. Clinical research demonstrates anti-inflammatory, neuroprotective, cardioprotective, and potential anti-senescent effects. Bioavailability is formulation-dependent; trans form is significantly more active than cis-resveratrol.
mechanisms: SIRT1 activation — mimics caloric restriction signaling, promotes mitochondrial biogenesis
AMPK activation — improves cellular energy sensing and glucose metabolism
mTOR inhibition — supports autophagy and cellular quality control
NF-kB and COX-2 inhibition — reduces pro-inflammatory signaling
benefits: Supports healthy aging at the cellular level
Supports mitochondrial function and energy production
Supports cardiovascular terrain and healthy circulation
safetyProfile: Well tolerated at 100–500mg/day. At very high doses (>2.5g/day) some reports of GI discomfort. Trans-resveratrol from P. cuspidatum is the well-studied, stable form.
contraindications: Estrogen-sensitive conditions — mild estrogenic and anti-estrogenic activity depending on tissue and dose
Caution during pregnancy — insufficient safety data
High doses pre-surgery — theoretical antiplatelet activity
drugInteractions: Anticoagulants — additive antiplatelet effect; monitor INR with warfarin
CYP1A2 and CYP3A4 substrates — resveratrol inhibits these enzymes at high doses
Chemotherapy — discuss with oncologist; resveratrol may modulate efficacy of certain cytotoxic agents
physicianNotes: Of significant interest in longevity medicine and metabolic syndrome protocols. SIRT1/AMPK activation makes it appropriate in pre-diabetic metabolic support. Monitor in patients on anticoagulation. In oncology, timing relative to cytotoxic treatments requires oncologist input.
patientGuidance: Take with a fat-containing meal for best absorption. If you are on blood thinners, diabetes medications, or cancer treatment, inform your physician.
